This isn't quite what this function is supposed to do as the output in your examples doesn't match the corresponding function calls in Lodash's `.sortedIndexOf`:

```javascript
> var lo = require('lodash');
> lo.sortedIndexOf([10, 5, 7, 3], 3)
-1
> lo.sortedIndexOf([10, 10, 5, 7, 3], 10)
-1
> lo.sortedIndexOf([10, 5, 7, 3], 11)
-1
```

If you look at the Lodash source, then you can see that Python equivalent is basically:

```python
index = sorted_index(array, value)
if index < len(array) and array[index] == value:
    return index
else:
    return -1
```

* https://github.com/lodash/lodash/blob/master/sortedIndexOf.js
* https://github.com/lodash/lodash/blob/master/sortedIndex.js
* https://github.com/lodash/lodash/blob/master/.internal/baseSortedIndex.js

My concern is e.g.:
db is upgraded
task is tried 3 times, fails all times.
task is cleared in the UI
task is tried 1 time, fails // ti.tries is now 4, max_attempts is 6 ti.task.retries is 3
db is downgraded 
Although the task should be retried two more times, it will not be retried because ti.tries > ti.task.retries is 3 (which is actually impossible in the old db version which could potentially cause other issues too).

My understanding is that checking hostname is not enough. There is only one task instance record with dag_id, task_id and execution date in DB. Once task re-attempts, the hostname information might be updated. So even if the task instance currently has hostname A, it could still have older logs with hostname B or C that got wiped out when DB updates. So this local machine might have older logs. 

Direct updating on top of existing DB records in generally makes things much harder to keep track. I would prefer having another table to keep track of task_instance changes.

Another common testing convention followed in this repo is to import whatever you're testing in the smallest scope possible. The way I'd do this is to make a method like:

```
@classmethod
```

`tries` is incremented every time there is a failure and when it reaches the limit, but never decremented, so if someone is using the driver for enough time to accumulate enough errors, `get_connection` will always return `None`.

In general, I think it's difficult to use a counter to decide if the network is unresponsive or not. Let's say you have 4 nodes, and one of them is always failing (but the other 3 are fine). After some requests, `tries` will reach `max_tries` and `get_connection` will return you `None`, even if the network is responsive.

An idea would be to count how many nodes have been `DELAY`ed, and if all nodes were unavailable, then raise an exception and reset the nodes `DELAY`s. What do you think?

I will either have the `iso8601` to be in `graphql/core/pyutils/iso8601.py` and import from there, or to require a generic version of `iso8601` in setup.py (generic version == no version specified).

The reason for that is any other package could be already using an older or newer version of `iso8601` and when installing `graphene` package it will trigger a incompatibility error as requires a specific version of it. (That's the reason Django includes a lot of third-party libraries in their own codebase).

So, to be clear. What I'm proposing here is different from what the current implementation is. In the current implementation, a handler will rerun in the next hook if its preconditions are still true. I'm proposing to never rerun the handler if the preconditions stay true. If the preconditions become false again and the become true again, we rerun the handler.

This is mostly to keep the model as simple as possible to stop the confusion about when handlers will be rerun.

@lukesneeringer Is there a reason you used `%r` instead of `%s` here? There is actually no distinction for protobuf types:

```python
>>> from google.cloud.pubsub_v1 import types
>>> msg = types.PubsubMessage(data=b'a', message_id=b'123f')
>>> str(msg)
'data: "a"\nmessage_id: "123f"\n'
>>> repr(msg)
'data: "a"\nmessage_id: "123f"\n'
>>> msg
data: "a"
message_id: "123f"

>>>
```
